title: Cat Bloody Discharge From Nipples: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about bloody nipple discharge in cats, including potential causes like mastitis or tumors, and why a vet visit is needed within 24-48 hours.
content_html:
TL;DR: Bloody nipple discharge in cats is usually caused by infection, inflammation, or tumors and requires a veterinary exam within 24–48 hours. Seek emergency care immediately if your cat has a high fever, lethargy, or if the mammary glands appear purple and feel hard.

What causes bloody nipple discharge in my cat?

Finding bloody or pink-tinged fluid leaking from your cat's nipples can be alarming. This symptom, known as serosanguinous discharge, typically indicates inflammation, infection, or abnormal tissue growth within the mammary glands. Common causes include Mastitis (a bacterial infection of the milk duct), Mammary Hyperplasia (rapid growth of the gland often seen in young, unspayed females), or Mammary Neoplasia (tumors). In some cases, it may also result from physical trauma to the chest area.

Is bloody nipple discharge in a cat an emergency?

  • The urgency level for bloody nipple discharge is classified as Medium, requiring a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours.
  • While rarely a "stop everything" emergency, you must contact an emergency clinic immediately if your cat exhibits signs of septic mastitis.
  • Immediate emergency signs include extreme lethargy, a high fever, or mammary glands that are hot, hard, and purple.

How does taking a photo of the discharge help my veterinarian?

  • A clear, high-resolution photo is vital because cats are fastidious groomers and may lick away the discharge before you reach the clinic.
  • Photos allow the vet to see the exact color, consistency, and volume of the fluid.
  • Capturing the appearance of the surrounding skin helps the veterinarian narrow down the diagnosis much faster.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

The occurrence of blood in milk, manifesting as pink or red-tinged fluid, can be attributed to the rupture of mammary blood vessels, often associated with udder swelling from edema or trauma. Frank blood in a single quarter is typically indicative of severe, acute mastitis or trauma, warranting the discontinuation of milking until the hemorrhage is controlled; intramammary antibiotics should be considered if mastitis is suspected. In post-partum queens, evaluation of milk from each gland is warranted with systemic illness.

Chapter: Surgery, Infectious Diseases, Hematology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1389)

faqs.question: Can unspayed cats get nipple discharge?
faqs.answer: Yes, unspayed cats are at a higher risk for mammary hyperplasia and mammary tumors due to hormonal fluctuations, both of which can cause fluid leakage.

faqs.question: Is mammary discharge in cats usually cancer?
faqs.answer: While discharge can be caused by infection, approximately 85-90% of mammary masses in cats are malignant, making professional evaluation essential to rule out cancer.

faqs.question: What will a vet do to diagnose the cause?
faqs.answer: Your vet will likely perform a physical exam, and may recommend a fine-needle aspirate (FNA), cytology of the fluid, or blood work to check for signs of infection.
